critique.summary: Scientists have discovered all five canonical nucleobases essential for DNA and RNA in samples returned from the asteroid Ryugu, supporting theories that asteroids contributed to the prebiotic chemistry leading to life on Earth. This finding aligns with previous discoveries from asteroid Bennu and certain meteorites, indicating these organic molecules are widespread in the solar system.
mainPoints: Samples from asteroid Ryugu contain all five nucleobases: uracil, adenine, guanine, cytosine, and thymine.
The presence of these nucleobases supports the hypothesis that carbonaceous asteroids contributed to the prebiotic chemical inventory of early Earth.
Similar nucleobases were previously found in samples from asteroid Bennu and meteorites like Orgueil and Murchison.
A unique correlation was found between nucleobase ratios and ammonia concentration, suggesting a previously unrecognized formation pathway.
The discovery does not imply life existed on Ryugu but indicates primitive asteroids could produce and preserve molecules important for the origin of life.
critique.truthfulness: The article is based on a peer-reviewed study published in Nature Astronomy and includes statements from multiple scientists, including the study's lead author and independent experts, which supports its accuracy. The findings about nucleobases in Ryugu samples are consistent with previous research on Bennu and meteorites, making the claims credible and well-supported by scientific evidence. The article responsibly clarifies that the presence of these molecules does not mean life existed on the asteroid, avoiding sensationalism.
considerations: The article does not discuss the specific chemical processes or environmental conditions on Ryugu that might lead to nucleobase formation in detail.
It does not explore alternative hypotheses about the origin of life beyond the asteroid delivery theory.
The implications for the broader search for life in the universe or on other celestial bodies are not elaborated.
Potential contamination or terrestrial alteration of samples, while likely addressed in the original study, is not mentioned in the article.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the significance of finding DNA and RNA building blocks in Ryugu asteroid samples, debating the origin of life and the implications of these findings. Some commenters question the novelty and importance of the discovery, while others highlight the scientific rigor and potential impact on understanding life's origins.

comments.author: johnsmalles
comments.text: Fascinating that all five nucleobases were found in Ryugu samples. The fact that these formed abiotically in an asteroid environment strengthens the case that the building blocks of life are common throughout the solar system. The amino acid findings from the same samples were already compelling, but having the complete nucleobase set is a different level of evidence.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately summarizes the scientific significance of the findings, emphasizing the abiotic formation of nucleobases and their implications for astrobiology.

comments.author: pfdietz
comments.text: It contains nucleobases.  But does it contain ribose, or ribose linked to the nucleobases, or to phosphates?  And more generally, does it also contain a grab bag of related chemicals that are not building blocks?  The existence of such blocks as minor constituents of a soup of random chemicals doesn't mean much, especially as the concentration of any such constituent declines exponentially with its complexity.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced critique, pointing out that the presence of nucleobases alone is insufficient to confirm the presence of complete nucleotides or meaningful prebiotic chemistry.

comments.author: 0x000xca0xfe
comments.text:
One longstanding theory is that life first began on Earth when asteroids carrying fundamental elements crashed into our planet long ago.
I'm no expert but this sounds strange. Surely those fundamental elements would also form in vast quantities on their own on an entire planet with volcanoes and oceans? Wouldn't a couple asteroids be the literal drop in the ocean in comparison?

The missing part is how do they form self-replicating mechanisms capable of evolution. I doubt an asteroid with a bit of organic dust is enough for that. If such small amounts suffice we should see the formation of new life forms from scratch, today, left and right I think?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid point about the relative contribution of asteroids versus endogenous planetary chemistry in the origin of life, though it underestimates the role of extraterrestrial delivery in prebiotic chemistry.

comments.author: Amorymeltzer
comments.text:
I'm in the middle of reading Peter Brannen's The Story of CO2 Is the Story of Everything—it's excellent and goes deep into the (bio)geochemistry of Earth—and he presents a good case for a metabolism-first development of life, taking advantage of "a disequilibrium that needed to be relieved at the vents, an unending stream d free energy to dissipate," rather than the RNA information-first theories.

It fits his overall narrative but it was an interesting way to think about life "as a thermodynamically necessary mechanism to relieve the continuous production of free geochemical energy on Earth... more efficiently than abiotic processes could." (Brannen quoting complex-systems scientist Anne-Marie Grisogono) I highly recommend the book.
comments.analysis: Provides a relevant alternative hypothesis on the origin of life, enriching the discussion with a well-regarded scientific perspective.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text:
That's not really new. It seems as if some people try to project "there is life outside of planet Earth". Well, the thing is ... is this question important? You already have life here. Synthetic biology will also progress. So why is it important if life is anywhere else? I don't understand it.

There is nothing magic in RNA or DNA. Granted, right now we can not easily explain how life gets "bootstrapped", but recently there was a paper of self-propagating RNA even of a kind of semi-random sequence; this RNA can just amplify itself. I am sure you can find many more similar examples eventually as well as biochemical reaction processes that can be "bootstrapped" - and I am also sure none of these work on an asteroid. So why is there this strange focus on "life outside of planet Earth"? Some people want research money, that is clear now.
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism about the importance of extraterrestrial life research, mixing valid points about synthetic biology with a somewhat cynical tone.

comments.author: stevenjgarner
comments.text:
Ummm ... the "Victoria University of Wellington in Australia"? Please. Victoria University is located in Wellington, New Zealand [1]. Nothing to do with Australia. Dr. Morgan Cable is a Senior Lecturer in Space Science at Te Herenga Waka, Victoria University of Wellington in New Zealand [2]. Can't believe that phys.org would publish such an error.

[1] https://www.wgtn.ac.nz/

[2] https://www.psi.edu/staff/profile/morgan-cable/
comments.analysis: Corrects a factual error about institutional location, contributing useful factual clarification.

comments.author: fusslo
comments.text:
I wonder how they prevent contamination of the containers used to collect and store samples.

I assume they have to be ultra clean in every sense of the word 'clean' with the cavity pulled to a vacuum. And also the equipment that collects the sample and puts it into the canister has to be clean as well.

The logistics aren't obvious to me at all
comments.analysis: Raises a practical and relevant question about contamination control in sample collection, highlighting an important aspect of astrobiological research.

comments.author: qsera
comments.text:
Doesn't multi-world interpretation pretty much answer how life originated?

I mean, even if the starting state require to bootstrap life have impossibly low chance to happen random, multi-world interpretation implies that there will be some worlds where it happened, and observation of life is only possible in such worlds..
comments.analysis: Applies the multiverse concept to life’s origin, which is speculative and not widely accepted as an explanatory mechanism in origin-of-life science.

comments.author: _ink_
comments.text: Are these building blocks not evaporated on impact?
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant question about the survivability of organic molecules during asteroid impact, though it lacks detail or further elaboration.

comments.author: hmokiguess
comments.text: How are samples collected? In space or as debris?
comments.analysis: Asks a basic but important procedural question about sample collection, indicating curiosity but limited depth.
